title: String enums — readable constants
instruction:
String enums assign explicit string values to each member. They appear as readable strings in logs and JSON responses — far more useful than numeric enums.

Create an order status enum.
successMessage: String enum values appear as-is in logs and JSON. The `switch` statement is exhaustive — TypeScript knows every possible `OrderStatus` value, so there's no need for a `default` case.

title: Numeric enums — automatic values
instruction:
Numeric enums auto-assign incrementing values starting at 0. They support reverse mapping — you can look up the name from the value.

Build a severity level enum for logging.
successMessage: Numeric enum values can be compared with `>=`. Reverse mapping (`LogLevel[level]`) gives you the name from the number — only available with numeric enums, not string enums.

title: Const enums — zero-overhead enums
instruction:
`const enum` is completely erased at compile time — TypeScript replaces every usage with the literal value. This eliminates the runtime object but removes reverse mapping.

Use const enums for HTTP status codes.
successMessage: Const enums compile to their literal values — `HttpStatus.OK` becomes `200` in the output. Zero runtime overhead. The trade-off: no reverse mapping and can't be used across package boundaries.

title: Enums vs union types — when to use each
instruction:
Many TypeScript teams prefer union types over enums for simplicity. Here's a comparison — when does each approach shine?

Compare an enum vs a union type for the same use case.
successMessage: Both work. Enum advantage: callers write `DirectionEnum.North` — no way to typo it. Union advantage: simpler, no extra syntax, `"NORTH"` is directly assignable. Most modern TypeScript uses union types for simple cases.

title: Enums as function parameters
instruction:
Enums serve as descriptive parameters that prevent magic strings or magic numbers. They make function calls self-documenting.

Build a report generator with enum parameters.
successMessage: Function calls read like English: `generateReport(ReportFormat.PDF, DateRange.Month, "Revenue")`. Swapping `format` and `range` would be a type error — both have different enum types even though they're both strings.

title: Enum member expressions
instruction:
Numeric enum members can be computed using bit flags. This is a classic pattern for representing combinations of options (like file permissions).

Build a permission system using bit-flag enums.
successMessage: Bit flags let you combine permissions with `|` and check them with `&`. A single number can represent multiple permissions simultaneously — `3` (binary `11`) means read AND write.

title: Iterating over enum values
instruction:
You can iterate over a numeric enum's values, but it requires care because numeric enums have both forward and reverse mappings.

Iterate over enum values correctly.
successMessage: Numeric enums have reverse mappings — `Object.values()` returns both `1, 2, 3, 4, 5` AND `"Monday", "Tuesday", ...`. Filtering to only numbers gives you the enum values. String enums don't have this issue.

title: Ambient enums — working with external definitions
instruction:
When declaring types for external JavaScript code (like a third-party library), you use `declare enum` to describe enums that exist at runtime but weren't written in TypeScript.

Simulate an ambient enum for an external library.
successMessage: `declare enum` is used in `.d.ts` declaration files to describe enums from JavaScript libraries. At runtime, the enum object is provided by the library. TypeScript just adds type checking around it.

title: Enum in a class — bundling related constants
instruction:
Enums can be declared inside or alongside classes to bundle related constants. This creates a clean namespace.

Build a User class with an inline Status enum.
successMessage: Bundling constants inside a class namespace prevents pollution of the global scope. `User.Status.Active` is more readable than a standalone `UserStatus.Active` enum. The `as const` makes the values literal types.

title: Enum exhaustiveness check
instruction:
One of the most powerful uses of enums is exhaustiveness checking — TypeScript can verify that every enum value is handled in a switch statement.

Build an action handler with exhaustive checking.
successMessage: The `default: never` pattern ensures every `ActionType` value is handled. Add a new value to `ActionType` and forget to handle it — TypeScript errors on the `never` line. This is the Redux pattern, and it works perfectly with TypeScript enums.

title: Map with enum keys — type-safe lookup tables
instruction:
Using an enum as the key type in a `Map` or `Record` creates exhaustive lookup tables where TypeScript ensures every enum value has an entry.

Build a configuration table using enum keys.
successMessage: `Record<Environment, Config>` requires a config entry for EVERY environment. Add a new `Environment.Testing` value and TypeScript immediately tells you to add its config. Zero chance of a missing environment.

title: Enum values as object keys with computed properties
instruction:
Enum members can be used as computed property names in object literals. This creates type-safe mappings between enum values and any data.

Map enum values to icons and descriptions.
successMessage: The `Record<NotificationType, { icon; color }>` requires all four notification types. Each maps to its own style. The icon emoji may render slightly differently depending on your environment.
